perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this MNDA without cause upon five (5) days written notice given to the other, provided that confidentiality obligations under Section A of this Attachment A shall survive termination hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this MNDA without cause upon one (1) day written notice given to the other, provided that confidentiality obligations under Section A of this Attachment A shall survive termination hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to one day may be considered unreasonable and contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in Massachusetts contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith requires parties to act honestly and fairly in their dealings. A one-day notice period could be seen as a breach of this duty, particularly if it causes significant harm to the other party, as it doesn't provide adequate time to prepare for the termination of the agreement. While 93A primarily deals with consumer protection, the underlying principle of fair dealing applies to commercial contracts as well.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the termination notice period from five days to one. The Perturbation Explanation asserts this may contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Massachusetts contract law. The Law Explanation expands, arguing a one-day notice could breach this duty if it causes significant harm due to inadequate preparation time. The provided law snippet from Chapter 93A, while primarily focused on consumer protection, touches on fair business practices, suggesting an underlying principle of fair dealing that applies to commercial contracts. While 93A may not be directly applicable, the underlying principle is. A one-day notice period could plausibly be considered a breach of good faith, making the modified clause potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Consistent with other provisions herein, each party assures that it will not knowingly, without obtaining prior authorization from the U.S. Dept. of Commerce Office of Export Administration, transmit directly or indirectly the technical data received pursuant hereto or the immediate product (including processes and services) produced directly by use of such technical data to Afghanistan, People's Republic of China, or any other Country Group Q, S, W, T, or Z country specified in Supplement No. I to Part 370 of U.S. Dept. of Commerce Export Administration Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Consistent with other provisions herein, each party assures that it will not knowingly, without obtaining prior authorization from the U.S. Dept. of Commerce Office of Export Administration, transmit directly or indirectly the technical data received pursuant hereto or the immediate product (including processes and services) produced directly by use of such technical data to North Korea, Cuba, or Iran.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting only North Korea, Cuba, and Iran, the modified text fails to include all countries currently under comprehensive U.S. export controls, potentially leading to violations of export control regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR comprehensively regulates exports and re-exports of goods, software, and technology. The original clause references specific country groups that were relevant in 1996. The updated clause lists only North Korea, Cuba, and Iran, omitting other countries subject to U.S. export controls. This omission creates a contradiction, as it implies that exports to other restricted countries are permissible, which violates the EAR.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by narrowing the scope of export-controlled countries. The original text refers to specific country groups defined in the U.S. Dept. of Commerce Export Administration Regulations (EAR). By contrast, the changed text lists only North Korea, Cuba, and Iran, thus omitting other countries that are actually subject to U.S. export controls. The Law Explanation states that this omission implies that exports to other restricted countries are permissible, which violates the EAR. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the EAR and Subchapter C, Export Administration Regulations, indicating the breadth and importance of these regulations. The altered contract language weakens the original obligation and could lead to non-compliance with U.S. export regulations, as the altered text doesn't include all countries under U.S. export controls.

perturbation.original_text: This document and appendices contain the entire agreement between the Parties and supersede any previous oral or written understandings, commitments or agreements pertaining to the subject matter hereof. This MNDA shall not be modified or changed in any manner except in a writing signed by both parties. If a court of competent jurisdiction finds any of the provisions hereto so over-broad as to be unenforceable, such provisions may be reduced in scope by the court to the extent it deems necessary to render the provision reasonable and enforceable.
perturbation.changed_text: This document and appendices contain the entire agreement between the Parties and supersede any previous oral or written understandings, commitments or agreements pertaining to the subject matter hereof. This MNDA shall not be modified or changed in any manner, even in a writing signed by both parties. If a court of competent jurisdiction finds any of the provisions hereto so over-broad as to be unenforceable, such provisions may NOT be reduced in scope by the court to the extent it deems necessary to render the provision reasonable and enforceable.
perturbation.explanation: By stating the MNDA cannot be modified even in writing, and that an unenforceable provision cannot be reduced by the court, it goes against the principle of contract modification and severability laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 158 (and related sections) of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts outlines the principles of contract modification, allowing parties to alter their agreements by mutual consent, usually in writing. Furthermore, severability clauses allow courts to modify overly broad or illegal provisions to make them enforceable. Prohibiting any modification and preventing judicial reduction of scope directly contradicts these fundamental principles of contract law, potentially rendering the entire agreement unenforceable. Although Massachusetts law relies heavily on common law regarding contracts, the Restatement is a persuasive source of authority.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the MNDA cannot be modified even in writing and that an unenforceable provision cannot be reduced by the court. This directly contradicts the principle of contract modification and severability, as the law explanation describes the Restatement (Second) of Contracts which allows parties to alter agreements by mutual consent and severability clauses which allow courts to modify provisions to make them enforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this by stating that a contract is an agreement between parties creating mutual obligations and that private law includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises, implying these terms can be modified if all parties agree. The changed text attempts to remove the ability to modify or sever which creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This MNDA shall be construed, interpreted and applied in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: This MNDA shall be construed, interpreted and applied in accordance with the laws of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of the People's Republic of China could create issues with enforceability in U.S. courts, especially given potential conflicts with U.S. law and public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Public Policy Exception to Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws Section 90 states that a choice of law provision will not be enforced if it violates a fundamental principle of public policy in the forum state (here, presumably a U.S. court). Applying PRC law might conflict with U.S. intellectual property rights, freedom of speech, or other fundamental legal principles. Although the Restatement is not binding, Massachusetts courts regularly use it in their decisions. This contradiction could make the agreement unenforceable in the US.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Massachusetts to the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation indicates this could violate public policy as per Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws Section 90, potentially conflicting with US intellectual property rights or freedom of speech. While there are no law snippets, the Perturbation and Law explanations sufficiently demonstrate that the change could make the agreement unenforceable in the US due to conflicts with fundamental legal principles. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.
